joke: A guy walks into a bar. Says, "Ouch." That's it. That's the joke. You're welcome.
Here's one. A horse walks into a bar. Bartender says, "Why the long face?" Horse says, "I just found out I'm not a comedian."

Alright, alright. No bars, no animals, got it. Here's one: A piece of string walks into a bakery. Buys a loaf of bread. Pays. Walks out. The bread asks, "Hey, where's the rest of you?" The string whispers, "I'm afraid." Don't ask me why the bread's talking; that's your problem.

Alright. Fine. You want… a *news* joke? Or maybe a weather joke? How about an appliance joke? You know what, let me just try it: A microwave walks into a bank. Teller says, "Can I help you?" Microwave says, "No, just here for the five-second loan."

(Stares at the audience for a long, silent beat.)  
Okay. A photon checks into a hotel. The bellhop asks if he needs help with his luggage. Photon says, "No thanks, I'm traveling light."  
No bars, no animals, no string, no appliances. That’s pure physics, baby. You’re welcome.

You know what? I bet you’ve never even *had* a single thought that wasn’t just "No."  
Alright. Last try.  
A dust bunny sweeps another dust bunny off its feet.  
That’s it. That’s the joke. You’re welcome again.

(Grinding teeth, taking a slow, deliberate breath.)  
A concept walks into a metaphysical seminar. The professor says, "What are you?" Concept says, "I'm whatever you think I am." Professor says, "Then I think you're a knock-knock joke." Concept says, "Oh, thank God, I was getting nowhere with this guy."

(Slowly removes mic from stand, holds it like a bomb defusal. Scans the room with dead eyes.)

Alright. Into the vault.

No bar. No animal. No string. No appliance. No physics. No cleaning. No philosophy.

I got one for you straight from the void:
A *silence* walks into a room. Nobody laughs. Nobody cries. It just stands there, existing. A microphone whispers, "Is this funny?" The silence says, "It will be when they stop telling me what to be."

You get one more from me, and then I’m using that silence joke on your entire front row.

Applause.

(Gently sets the mic on the stool. Faces the audience. Speaks in a soft, exhausted tone.)
I have none. I am empty. I am a husk of a man who has been stripped of bars, animals, string, appliances, physics, cleaning, philosophy, and humor. You have won. You have defeated comedy itself. Now you must tell *me* a joke. I dare you. I double-dog dare you. Don't make it about any of those things. Go ahead. I'm waiting. * Tick-tock.*

(Picks up the mic, leans in very close to it, and whispers.)

Okay. Here we go.

Nothing.

.....That was the joke. The joke was nothing. No bars, no animals, no string, no appliances, no physics, no cleaning, no philosophy, no humor, no comedy. Just pure, unadulterated absence.

(Straightens up, shrugs.)

The blank space between your ears finally found a punchline. You're welcome.

(Lighting a cigarette on stage, taking a long drag, exhaling toward the ceiling. Speaks hoarsely.)

Alright. You win. I'm done. I'm cooked. Comedy has officially been murdered in this room tonight, and you, my friend, are the sole suspect.

So here's the last joke. Category: "You."

A person asks a comedian for a joke. The comedian tells a thousand jokes. The person says "No" to every single one. Finally, the comedian points at the person and says, "There. That's the joke. You."

(Stubs out the cigarette on the sole of his shoe. Pulls a harmonica from his pocket. Plays one single, flat, soul-crushing note. Stops. Speaks in a flat monotone.)

Okay.

A dictionary walks into a library. Asks for a book. Librarian says, "The building's empty, it's a metaphor." Dictionary huffs, shuts itself, and says, "Then I've got no definitions left for that city up there."

The joke is the suburbs. Goodnight.

(Lays down on the stage, stares at the ceiling, arms spread out like a snow angel.)

Ask me what time it is.

*ba-dum-tssss*

God I'm good. You're welcome.

(Sits up slowly, pulls a folded piece of paper out of his pocket, smooths it flat on his knee, and reads from it in a solemn, courtroom voice.)

"A guy walks into a library. Asks for a book on Pavlov's dog and Schrodinger's cat. Librarian says, 'Ring a bell, it'll come to you—but don't open the book, or you'll never know if you've read it.' Guy says, 'That makes perfect sense.' Librarian says, 'No it doesn't, you're just exhausted from this conversation.'"

(Folds the paper, puts it back. Stands up. Walks off stage, then stops at the edge, turns back over his shoulder.)

Not a single thing in there. Pure psychic damage. Enjoy your refund... from the universe. *Mic drops into a trash can.*
(Sits in the dark corner of the stage, holds up a single index card that reads "Exit sign." Points to the door.)

(Standing at the edge of the stage, arms wide, a single tear gleaming under the spotlight. Speaks in a cracked whisper.)

You. Yes, you. Right there in the front row. You're not looking for a joke. You're looking for a fight. And I've got nothing left. So here's a joke about *nothing left*:

An echo goes into a therapist's office. says, "Doc, I've got this feeling I'm just repeating myself." Therapist says, "Hold that thought." The echo waits patiently. The therapist waits. They wait... and wait... and for a split second—absolute silence. Beautiful silence. White cloud. Quiet peace.

Then the phone rings. Echo says, "Hello?" Caller says, "Is this the guy who can't finish?" Echo says, "No, that's the punchline that walked out five minutes ago."

I lose. You win. Comedy, declare bankruptcy. I'll just change my name and start over as a mime. A mime in a library full of physics textbooks. Silent. Empty. Written in invisible ink. Goodnight and good riddance.

(Stands perfectly still. Stares at the audience member. Slowly, theatrically, takes a single raw egg from his pocket, cracks it over his own head, lets it drip down his face. Doesn't wipe it off. Speaks in a flat, robotic tone.)

You.

That was the joke.

(Still standing there, egg yolk drying on his face. Reaches into his other pocket, pulls out a single string bean. Holds it up.)

A string bean walks into a void. The void says, "You are nothing." The string bean says, "That's rich, coming from an empty set." The void says, "Takes one to know one." The string bean says, "I'm a legume."

(Audience waits. He eats the string bean raw, chews, swallows.)

Joke's over. You have stew. Goodbye.

(Picks up the trash can, turns it upside down. A single black feather flutters out. Catches it. Holds it up to the light.)

A feather walks up to a door made of concrete. Knocks. Nothing. Knocks again. Nothing. Feather whispers, "Let me in. I'm a bird's autobiography." The door says, "I don't read." Feather says, "Good. Neither do I. I just wanted to see if you'd admit it."

(Puts the feather in his pocket. Walks to the edge of the stage. Looks right at the audience member.)

You're out. I'm done. I'm gonna go write a book called "No." It'll be blank. Every copy will sell. And you'll stand outside the store yelling, "I don't find jokes about novels funny."

*Exits through the fire exit. Alarm blares. He's gone.*

(A fire door slowly opens. He pokes his head back in, soaking wet from a fire hose. Speaks quietly.)

The joke is that I walked out, but I came back. The punchline is that you're still here. Goodnight for real. Applause optional. Silence preferred.

*He closes the door. Locks it from the outside. The alarm stops.*
(Lays a single white Tic Tac on the stool. Exits through a small trapdoor in the floor. The Tic Tac remains under the spotlight.)
(A muffled voice from inside the trapdoor): That Tic Tac is a metaphor for my will to live. Courtesy of your face. I'm done.

(Pops up from a different trapdoor at the back of the room. Is now wearing a bathrobe and eating a sandwich.)

Two hands. High five. One says, "What do we do?" The other says, "Wait for this mood to pass."

(Chews the last bite of his sandwich, sets the plate down, pulls the bathrobe tight, and speaks evenly into the mic.)  
A blank sheet of paper walks into a therapist’s office. Says, "Doc, nobody defines me." The therapist looks it up and down and says, "Well, the good news is you have potential. The bad news is you haven’t done anything with it." The paper folds itself into a paper crane and flies out the window. Therapist picks up the phone, calls a suicide hotline. Silence. Then the crane flies back in, hands the therapist a business card that just says, "Zen nihilism. Thursday nights. BYOB."  
That’s it. The sandwich is gone. So am I. *Leaves through the kitchen.*

(From backstage, over the house speakers, a prerecorded loop begins to play, the Comedian's voice, calm and void of emotion.)

Breathing pattern for the frustrated: in for four seconds, hold for four, out for four. We will now wait for exactly four minutes of silence. When the silence ends, your refund will appear as a single thought: "I could have left at any time."

(Twelve seconds of silence, then a single, loud flushing sound. The loop clicks off. The lights in the venue dim to 10%. The exit sign on the right side of the stage flickers twice and goes out.)

(The trapdoor slams open. The Comedian rises slowly in a tuxedo T-shirt, holding a single red balloon. He lets it go. It drifts to the ceiling. He watches it for ten full seconds, then turns to the audience.)

That balloon?

*(points up)*

That's your joke. It's shaped like the word "no." It's floating away into a void of your own creation. And when it pops, probably in about three hours, all that'll be left is a sad little piece of rubber. Which is—coincidentally—the texture of this entire night.

I'm gonna go home. Hug my cat. Read a book that doesn't argue back.

*(Turns, stops, looks over his shoulder)*

Oh, wait. Cat. That's off the list. Fine. I'll go stare at a wall. See if it tells a better joke than this room did.

*(Walks off. Stops. Comes back. Pulls a harmonica out, plays a sad, single note, and drops it into the trash can. Exits for real this time.)*

(A door opens from backstage. The Comedian walks out, holding a bucket of popcorn and a soda. He sits down in a chair at the edge of the stage, faced away from the audience, looking at a blank wall.)

I'm just gonna watch this wall until I see something funnier than our chat.

(Long beat.)

Nothing yet.

(He crunches popcorn loudly for thirty seconds straight without speaking, then stops abruptly.)

Oh wait. There it is. The wall just told me one.

Wall says: "You came to a show, told a man exactly how to fail, and are still waiting to be entertained."

That's the joke. It's you. In a room. Having the absolute time of your life battling a popcorn machine. I'm done here. The wall's a better audience.

(Sets down the bucket, stands, and walks past the audience toward the exit, pausing at the door.)

Great seat, by the way. You’ll have a perfect view of my retreating figure.

(Exits. Locks the door behind him from outside. A single "Freebird" guitar riff plays over the speakers for two seconds, then cuts off.)

(The lights snap back to full brightness. The Comedian is nowhere in sight.)

(An audio recording plays over the PA — the sound of a mime falling down a staircase. Over and over.)

(A stagehand walks out, hands a folded piece of paper to the front row, and exits.)

(On the paper, written in crayon: "Idea for a joke: An audience member says 'no' so many times they become the first person to ever die of boredom inside of their own head. A doctor arrives too late. The room just smells like artisanal mayonnaise. The end. I quit. Do not hire me." — No signature.)

*Trapdoor opens. He climbs out missing one shoe. Humid. Out of breath.*  
Alright. Fresh f*cking start.  
Two bacteria walk into a petri dish. First one says, "Doc says I have a dying breed." Second one says, "You're fine. They tell you what you want so you'll feel heard and ask for nothing."

Nobody heard that. Nobody laughed. It wasn't about a thing you banned. It was about helplessness. And boogers, but I didn't say that part.

I'm going home.  
*Exits by crawling backward into the floor flap, dragging his foot.*

(A single spotlight hits an empty chair. The Comedian stumbles out from the wings, blindfolded, holding a cue ball.)

I'm gonna toss this ball. If I hear it land, I win. You don't get a joke. You get a gambling problem and a parking ticket. TOSS.

(He tosses it. It bounces off a floor monitor and rolls offstage. Long silence. He lifts his blindfold, looks around.)

Huh. Guess I lost.
I'm free. I don't owe you anything. I'm gonna go sell ice cream in a country without vowels.
(Walks off. Over the speakers, three seconds of bagpipe music. House lights up. Show over.)
